steps: Express each concentration as a decimal: 2% = 0.02, 5% = 0.05, 3% = 0.03.
Compute the salt contributed by each solution: $x$ liters at 2% gives $0.02x$ liters of salt; $y$ liters at 5% gives $0.05y$ liters of salt.
The combined volume is $x + y$ liters at 3%, so the salt in the mixture is $0.03(x+y)$. Setting total salt in equal to total salt out: $0.02x + 0.05y = 0.03(x+y)$.

Evaluate every choice:
  • A: $0.02x + 0.05y = 3(x+y)$ — uses 3 instead of 0.03 on the right side, making the concentration 300%. Eliminated.
  • B: $0.02x + 0.05y = 0.03(x+y)$ — correctly balances salt in decimal form. That matches choice B — B is correct.
  • C: $0.2x + 0.5y = 3(x+y)$ — scales both sides up by a factor of 10 on the left but 100 on the right. Inconsistent. Eliminated.
  • D: $0.2x + 0.5y = 0.3(x+y)$ — represents 20%, 50%, and 30% concentrations, not the given values. Eliminated.
